params.text:
Protecting privacy during personal care

Personal care — washing, dressing, toileting, continence support — is where privacy is most easily lost and most keenly felt. A few consistent habits protect it every time:

  • Knock and wait for a response before entering someone's room, even if the door is open.
  • Close doors and draw curtains before you begin, and keep them closed throughout.
  • Cover the person with a towel or robe, exposing only the part of the body you are actively caring for.
  • Ask before you act — explain what you are about to do and gain agreement as you go.
  • Lower your voice so that conversations about the body or bodily functions are not overheard.

None of these take extra time once they become second nature — but each one tells the person that their body and their modesty are respected.

params.alt: Illustration for Privacy in personal care

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the sentence that describes good privacy practice during personal care.
params.textField: Always *knock* on the door and wait before entering. Draw the *curtains* and close the door before you start. Keep the person *covered* with a towel, exposing only what you are washing. *Explain* each step so they know what to expect, and speak *quietly* so private matters are not *overheard*.
